front: Federal agents arrest suspects, skip taking them before a U.S. Commissioner, and instead hold them in a barren cell for 14 hours of continuous questioning until they confess. Invoking its supervisory power (not the Constitution), which case excluded those confessions from federal court?
back:
**McNabb v. United States (1943)**
Holding: Confessions obtained from a federal arrestee during prolonged detention before presentment to a magistrate are inadmissible in federal court — an exclusionary rule grounded in the Supreme Court's supervisory power over federal criminal justice, not the Constitution.
Signature: to admit such evidence "would stultify the policy which Congress has enacted into law"; the Court expressly declined to reach the Fifth Amendment issue.
Why it matters: origin of the federal prompt-presentment (McNabb-Mallory) rule — a FEDERAL-only confession-suppression doctrine separate from voluntariness and from Miranda.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Miranda** (warnings) or the **voluntariness** due-process line — McNabb-Mallory turns on UNNECESSARY PRE-PRESENTMENT DELAY, and is a federal supervisory rule.

front: A federal arrestee is held and interrogated for hours instead of being taken "without unnecessary delay" before a committing magistrate as Fed. R. Crim. P. 5(a) requires; he then confesses. Which case held the confession must be suppressed because of the delay?
back:
**Mallory v. United States (1957)**
Holding: A confession obtained during "unnecessary delay" in bringing a federal arrestee before a magistrate (Fed. R. Crim. P. 5(a)) must be excluded; delay to extract a confession is forbidden, though a brief delay for quick verification may be permissible.
Signature: arraignment "without unnecessary delay" — "the delay must not be of a nature to give opportunity for the extraction of a confession."
Why it matters: the second half of the "McNabb-Mallory" rule — ties the federal prompt-presentment exclusion to Rule 5(a)'s text.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Miranda** (warnings) or **due-process voluntariness** — this is the federal DELAY rule; a confession can be voluntary yet still suppressed for presentment delay.

front: Which 2009 case held that 18 U.S.C. § 3501 modified but did NOT abolish the federal prompt-presentment rule — a voluntary confession within 6 hours of arrest is admissible, but beyond 6 hours the unnecessary-delay exclusionary rule still applies?
back:
**Corley v. United States (2009)**
Holding: "§ 3501 modified McNabb-Mallory without supplanting it" — under § 3501(c), a voluntary confession made within 6 hours of arrest is admissible despite presentment delay; a confession made beyond 6 hours is still subject to suppression if the delay was unnecessary or unreasonable.
Signature: the 6-hour safe harbor — reading § 3501(a) to abolish the rule would render § 3501(c) "nonsensical and superfluous."
Why it matters: the modern, controlling statement of the federal prompt-presentment rule; resolves the circuit split over § 3501's reach.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Miranda** (§ 3501 tried and failed to override Miranda; Dickerson handles that) — Corley is about the DELAY/presentment rule, not warnings or voluntariness.

front: At trial a defendant tells an exculpatory "frame-up" story for the first time; the prosecutor cross-examines him on why he stayed silent after getting his Miranda warnings at arrest. Which case held this violates due process?
back:
**Doyle v. Ohio (1976)**
Holding: Using a defendant's silence AFTER receiving Miranda warnings to impeach his trial testimony violates the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
Signature: Miranda warnings carry an "implicit" assurance that silence will carry no penalty, so post-warning silence is "insolubly ambiguous" and unfair to use against him.
Why it matters: the cornerstone limit on impeachment use of post-Miranda silence.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Harris v. New York** (un-Mirandized STATEMENTS may impeach a testifying defendant) or **Salinas v. Texas** (PRE-custody, pre-warning silence is usable) — Doyle is specifically about POST-warning SILENCE.

front: A defendant, with appointed counsel, invokes his Sixth Amendment right to counsel at a bail hearing on an armed-robbery charge; police later question him (after Miranda waivers) about an unrelated murder. Which case held that invoking 6A counsel did NOT invoke the 5A Miranda right for the uncharged crime?
back:
**McNeil v. Wisconsin (1991)**
Holding: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel is "offense-specific," and invoking it at a judicial proceeding (a bail hearing) does NOT invoke the non-offense-specific Fifth Amendment Miranda-Edwards right to counsel for other, uncharged offenses.
Signature: "offense-specific"; "Requesting the assistance of an attorney at a bail hearing" is not a request for counsel as to custodial interrogation.
Why it matters: the hinge separating the 5A and 6A counsel rights.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Arizona v. Roberson** (5A-Edwards invocation bars questioning even on UNRELATED crimes) or **Texas v. Cobb** (6A right is offense-specific by Blockburger) — McNeil keeps the two rights in separate lanes.

front: A jailhouse informant offers a frightened inmate protection from other prisoners in exchange for the truth, eliciting a murder confession later found coerced. Which case held that admitting a coerced confession is subject to harmless-error review, not automatic reversal?
back:
**Arizona v. Fulminante (1991)**
Holding: The admission of an involuntary/coerced confession is a "trial error" subject to harmless-error analysis under Chapman, not automatic reversal (though on these facts the error was NOT harmless and the conviction was reversed).
Signature: a coerced confession is a "trial error," "similar in both degree and kind to the erroneous admission of other types of evidence" — distinguished from "structural" defects.
Why it matters: overturned the old rule that a coerced confession always required reversal.
⚠ Don't confuse with the voluntariness/admissibility line (**Brown v. Mississippi, Ashcraft, Spano, Colorado v. Connelly**) — those decide WHETHER a confession comes in; Fulminante decides the consequence when a coerced one was wrongly admitted.

front: Police give Miranda warnings and get a waiver, then — without telling the suspect in advance — question him about a different, more serious crime (a homicide) than the one he expected. Which case held the waiver was still "knowing"?
back:
**Colorado v. Spring (1987)**
Holding: A Miranda waiver is "knowing and intelligent" even though police did not warn the suspect of all the crimes about which he would be questioned; awareness of every subject of interrogation is not a prerequisite to a valid waiver.
Signature: "The Constitution does not require that a criminal suspect know and understand every possible consequence of a waiver" of the Fifth Amendment privilege.
Why it matters: defines the "knowing" prong narrowly — police silence about the topic of questioning doesn't defeat a waiver.
⚠ Pair with **Moran v. Burbine** (waiver judged on the suspect's OWN awareness; police failure to tell him an attorney called does not vitiate it) — both reject outside-information theories of an invalid waiver.

front: During custodial interrogation a juvenile asks, "Can I have my probation officer here?" Which case held that request is NOT a per se invocation of the right to counsel/silence, so admissibility turns on the totality of the circumstances?
back:
**Fare v. Michael C. (1979)**
Holding: A juvenile's request to see his probation officer is NOT a per se invocation of his Fifth Amendment/Miranda rights (a probation officer is not the equivalent of an attorney); whether he validly waived his rights is judged by the totality of the circumstances.
Signature: admissibility is "resolved on the totality of the circumstances surrounding the interrogation," considering the juvenile's age, experience, and background.
Why it matters: limits what counts as an invocation and applies the totality test to juvenile waivers.
⚠ Don't confuse with **J.D.B. v. North Carolina** (a child's AGE is relevant to the CUSTODY analysis) — Fare is about INVOCATION/WAIVER, a different question; both involve juveniles but at different steps.
